MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Nov-08-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Wendy Chau, Esq. of the Law Offices of Wendy H. Chau, Tel. 415.539.6116, appearing in person for Plaintiff Jose C. Rodriguez, Jr.;
Jianlin Song, Esq. of Wilson Elser, Tel. 856.625.7966, appearing in person for Defendants HealthRight 360, HealthRight 360 Foundation, and Steve Garcia.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT HEALTHRIGHT 360 FOUNDATION'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

This matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant HealthRight 360 Foundation's (HRF) motion for summary judgment is granted.

Plaintiff Jose Rodriguez is suing HealthRight 360 (HR 360), a nonprofit that treats substance use disorder, for consumer and tort claims related to his stay at the facility and interactions with its employees. Rodriguez has named HRF as an additional defendant on grounds that HR 360 and HRF are one and the same entity. (Opp. 9:7-9.) Rodriguez intends to pierce the corporate veil in case HR 360 lacks funds to pay punitive damages. (Id.) HRF now moves for summary judgment.

To prevail on summary judgment, the movant must show there is no triable issue of material fact such that it is entitled to judgment as a matter of law. (CCP 437c.) A defendant can meet its burden by demonstrating the plaintiff cannot establish one or more elements of her cause of action. (Id.) The burden then shifts to the plaintiff to present evidence establishing a triable issue exists on one or more material facts. (Id.) The court liberally construes evidence and resolves doubts in the opponent's favor. (Ghazarian v. Magellan Health, Inc. (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 171, 182.)

HRF contends it is a nonprofit organization separate and distinct from HR 360 that was established for the purpose of providing funding. (Undisputed material facts "UMFs" 7-8.) As evidence, HRF submits the declaration of HRF officer Tony Duong who affirms the two organizations are distinct. (Duong decl. para. 4-5.) Rodriguez does not object to this evidence, and the burden shifts to him to establish a triable issue of material fact on this issue.

In opposition, Rodriguez cites the same Duong declaration, which states HRF was established to fund HR 360 but has not realized this goal. (UMF 7, Duong decl. par. 5.) Rodriguez also cites his attorney's declaration, in which she explains that she added HRF to the lawsuit because "HR360 might not have enough money in their insurance to pay." (Chau decl. para. 27.) Neither of these statements create a triable issue of material fact on whether the entities are distinct. Chau's speculation on how to satisfy judgment is irrelevant to this inquiry. Duong's statement that HRF was established to fund HR 360 does not show "there is such a unity of interest and ownership that separate personalities of corporation and individual no longer exist." (Mid-Century Ins. Co. v. Gardner (1992) 9 Cal.app.4th 1205, 1212.) Finally, HRF's objections to paragraph 27 of the Chau declaration are sustained: the website Chau references is not properly authenticated, judicially noticed, or cited in the separate statement. Accordingly, the motion for summary judgment is granted, and all evidentiary objections not ruled upon are preserved for appeal. (CCP 437c(q).)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. CRC 3.1312 compliance is required.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Matter Not Reported. Bailiff: Park. (301/RCE)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Nov-08-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Wendy Chau, Esq. of the Law Offices of Wendy H. Chau, Tel. 415.539.6116, appearing in person for Plaintiff Jose C. Rodriguez, Jr.;
Jianlin Song, Esq. of Wilson Elser, Tel. 856.625.7966, appearing in person for Defendants HealthRight 360, HealthRight 360 Foundation, and Steve Garcia.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT HEALTHRIGHT 360's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

This matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant HealthRight 360's (HR 360) motion for summary judgment, or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied.

Plaintiff Jose Rodriguez entered HR 360, a nonprofit rehabilitation facility that treats substance abuse disorder. Rodriguez alleges that the program manager, Steve Garcia, made misleading claims about HR 360, including that HR 360 offered vocational and educational opportunities. Rodriguez allegedly paid Garcia and Chi Wong, another HR 360 employee, thousands of dollars to receive these opportunities. Garcia and Wong allegedly injected fentanyl into Rodriguez and gave him alcohol. Rodriguez then sued Wong, Garcia, HR 360 and others for consumer and fraud claims. HR 360 now moves for summary adjudication on the first, third, fifth, and sixth causes of action (COAs) against it, as well as Rodriguez's request for punitive damages.

Inclusion of a fact in a separate statement concedes its materiality. (Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 252.) If a triable issue is raised as to any of the facts, the whole motion must be denied. (Salazar v. Thomas (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 467, 475 fn. 6.) Here, HR 360's separate statement fails to separately identify the undisputed material facts in support of each specific COA. (CRC, Rule 3.1350(b).) So, if any fact in HR 360's separate statement is disputed, the entire motion must be denied. That is the case here. Various material facts in HR 360's separate statement have been disputed with admissible evidence. For instance, in undisputed material fact "UMF" 1, HR 360 claims Rodriguez "never made a complaint about Garcia to Ms. Williams," the Vice President of Compliance. HR 360 cites the Williams deposition in which she states she does not recall an advocate telling her about Garcia and Wong stealing money. (UMF 91, Singh decl. Ex. 9, Williams depo. 59:2-10.) This evidence does not fully address UMF 91 because it omits whether Rodriguez complained directly to Williams about Garcia. But that aside, the evidence cited in opposition to UMF 1 shows Rodriguez did complain to Williams. Indeed, Rodriguez recounts a conversation where he "told her about the money, the whole situation" (UMF 91, Chau decl. Ex. 3, Rodriguez depo. 312: 4-6.) HR 360 does not object to this evidence. Having found at least one disputed material fact pertaining to all COAs, the entire motion must be denied.

Order signed.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Nov-08-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Wendy Chau, Esq. of the Law Offices of Wendy H. Chau, Tel. 415.539.6116, appearing in person for Plaintiff Jose C. Rodriguez, Jr.;
Jianlin Song, Esq. of Wilson Elser, Tel. 856.625.7966, appearing in person for Defendants HealthRight 360, HealthRight 360 Foundation, and Steve Garcia.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT STEVE GARCIA'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

This matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant Steve Garcia's motion for summary judgment, or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied.

Inclusion of a fact in a separate statement concedes its materiality. (Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 252.) If a triable issue is raised as to any of the facts, the whole motion must be denied. (Salazar v. Thomas (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 467, 475 fn. 6.) Here, Garcia's separate statement fails to separately identify the undisputed material facts in support of each specific COA. (CRC, Rule 3.1350(b).) So, if any fact in Garcia's separate statement is disputed, the entire motion must be denied. That is the case here. Various material facts in Garcia's separate statement have been disputed with admissible evidence. For instance, in undisputed material fact "UMF" #11, Garcia claims Rodriguez "could not recall any specific details about the alleged misrepresentations." The supporting evidence cited, however, indicates Rodriguez remembered some details, like the who, what, and when of these misrepresentations. Indeed, Rodriguez stated in his deposition that Garcia told him HR 360 has a school in around 2017 or 2018. ("UMF" #11; Singh decl. Ex. 5, p. 151:21-25; 152:1-20.) Further, the evidence cited in opposition to UMF #11 shows Rodriguez remembered additional details, i.e., that Garcia and Wang told Rodriguez they were his point of contact for the school. (Disputed fact #11, Chau decl. Ex. 3, p. 264:21-22.) Garcia does not object to this evidence. In sum, Garcia concedes it is material whether Rodriguez remembered details of the alleged misrepresentations. (Nazir supra, 178 Cal.App.4th at 252.) Garcia's own evidence cited in support of this fact shows Rodriguez remembered some details, and Rodriguez's evidence cited in opposition shows he remembered others. Having found at least one disputed material fact pertaining to all COAs, the entire motion must be denied.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Matter Not Reported. Bailiff: Park. (301/RCE)

Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Denying Spoliation
Off Calendar Aug-19-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF JOSE RODRIGUEZ JR's Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Denying Spoliation.

Plaintiff's "motion for reconsideration of order denying plaintiff's motion to compel and motion for spoliation" is denied.

A reconsideration motion must be based on "new or different facts, circumstances, or law" and a valid reason for not presenting them earlier. (CCP 1008; Gilberd v. AC Transit (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1500.) Plaintiff says the purported new facts were in Steve Garcia's June 6, 2024 deposition. However, Garcia was discussed in the May 13 initial hearing on plaintiff's two discovery motions, but then plaintiff did not depose Garcia for another 24 days. Moreover, the court's order on the two motions was not filed until June 10, so plaintiff still had four days to present his "new facts" to the court after the June 6 deposition (the ex parte calendar was available). In short, plaintiff shows no valid reason for failing to present his facts earlier. No sanctions are awarded.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

Motion For Reconsideration
Off Calendar Sep-19-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Wendy Ha Chau, Esq. (415) 539-6116 Law Offices of Wendy H. Chau for Plaintiff. (In-person)
Naina Singh, Esq. 415-554-3913 Deputy City Attorney for Defendant City and County of San Francisco et al.

ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF JOSE RODRIGUEZ JR'S Motion For Reconsideration.

Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration of the order granting summary judgment is denied.

Plaintiff's amended reconsideration memorandum is "based upon new facts." (Id. at 12:6-7.) But there is nothing new and/or relevant about the "facts" plaintiff asserts, and his counsel had more than two years to investigate and conduct discovery after filing suit.

Plaintiff's lead argument is that he cannot "get a hold of the County's operating manual," because "the County website's [sic] is down." (Memo. 12:17-20.) This is not a new fact and plaintiff had more than two years to get this manual.

Plaintiff next argues "HealthRight 360 should have filed a LIC 624 form" with a state agency. (Memo. 12:25.) This is not a new fact and it is not relevant to the summary judgment grounds for the City and County of San Francisco. (6/22/23 MSJ Order 2:2-3:9.)

Finally, plaintiff argues legal points. (Memo. 14:7-15:18.) These are not new facts - or, for that matter, new law.

Order is signed after the hearing.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Amended Notice Of Motion For New Trial
Off Calendar Sep-19-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Wendy Ha Chau, Esq. (415) 539-6116 Law Offices of Wendy H. Chau for Plaintiff. (In-person)
Naina Singh, Esq. 415-554-3913 Deputy City Attorney for Defendant City and County of San Francisco et al.

ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF JOSE RODRIGUEZ JR'S Amended Notice Of Motion For New Trial.

Plaintiff's motion for new trial is denied.

Plaintiff's amended memorandum argues the following grounds.

First, plaintiff says "the weight of the evidence" on the agency issue favors him. (Memo. 9:14, see also 14:7-15:2.) As set out in the summary judgment order, it does not. (Id. at 2:2-3:9.)

Second, plaintiff's counsel says she "was surprised that she mistakenly failed to file a separate statement," labeling the court not accepting a separate statement she filed nearly a month late "an irregularity." (Memo. 11:21-12:19.) The statute requires "ordinary prudence," absent here. (CCP 657(3).)

Third, plaintiff claims "newly discovered evidence." (Memo. 12:20-13:13.) Much of this is already addressed by the court's reconsideration rulings, which are incorporated by reference. And plaintiff's late-filed declarations are not newly discovered.

Fourth, Plaintiff asserts that "the damages the court awarded were excessive" (Memo. 14:1), but the court awarded no damages.

Fifth, plaintiff asserts "an error in law" (Memo. 15:3), but does not say what it supposedly was.

Order is signed after the hearing.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)
